title: On Optimal Scott Sentences of Finitely Generated Algebraic Structures
overall_summary: The paper studies the optimal infinitary Scott-sentence complexity of finitely generated structures, especially groups, by relating d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentences to the absence of proper existentially elementary self-copies. Its central characterization says that a finitely generated structure has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence exactly when it is not self-reflective, equivalently when the orbit of a generating tuple is Π^0_1-definable. The main technical method is an index-set construction: a self-reflective computable finitely generated structure has Σ^0_3 many-one complete index set, using a chain of existentially elementary self-copies and a priority-style collapse construction. The paper then constructs a finitely generated computable self-reflective group using small cancellation theory and an HNN extension, giving a finitely generated computable group with no d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the finite-generation/orbit-definability analysis: if a structure has a finite generating tuple with definable orbit, automorphisms are controlled by finite images, while self-reflective constructions show how existential self-similarity can obstruct low-complexity descriptions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let X be an oracle. Let I^X(A) be the set of indices e such that the X-oracle Turing machine Φ_e^X computes the atomic diagram of a countable structure isomorphic to A. If A has an X-computable Scott sentence of infinitary complexity Σ^0_α, respectively Π^0_α, respectively d-Σ^0_α, then I^X(A) belongs to the corresponding pointclass Σ^0_α(X), respectively Π^0_α(X), respectively d-Σ^0_α(X). Here d-Σ^0_α means a conjunction of a Σ^0_α sentence and a Π^0_α sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as the standard connection between Scott sentences and index sets. Given an index e for an X-computable atomic diagram, satisfaction of an X-computable L_{ω1ω} sentence of complexity Σ^0_α or Π^0_α by the structure computed by Φ_e^X has exactly the corresponding arithmetical/hyperarithmetical complexity relative to X, by induction on the formula complexity and the definitions of the infinitary hierarchy. Since a Scott sentence for A is true exactly in the copies of A, membership e ∈ I^X(A) is equivalent to satisfaction of that sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finitely generated countable structure. Say that a substructure B ⊆ M is Σ^0_1-elementary in M, written B ≺_1 M, if for every existential first-order formula φ and tuple b from B, M ⊨ φ(b) if and only if B ⊨ φ(b). Say that M is self-reflective if M contains a proper substructure B ⊊ M such that B ≺_1 M and B is isomorphic to M. Then the following are equivalent: (1) M has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence; (2) M is not self-reflective; (3) for all generating tuples of M, equivalently for some generating tuple of M, the automorphism orbit of that tuple is definable by a Π^0_1 formula.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper proves the equivalence of (1) and (2), while citing Alvir, Knight, and McCoy for the equivalence between (1) and (3). The implication from non-self-reflection to a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence is Theorem 2.1: choose a finite generating tuple g, collect the quantifier-free formulas witnessing that any tuple satisfying the atomic type of g but not generating the whole structure fails to be existentially elementary, and use these witnesses to build a Σ^0_2 plus Π^0_2 Scott sentence. Conversely, if M were self-reflective and had a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence, choose an oracle X computing the sentence and M. Proposition 1.2 would put the relativized index set I^X(M) in d-Σ^0_2(X), while Theorem 2.2 says that self-reflection makes I^X(M) Σ^0_3(X) many-one complete, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a finitely generated structure. If A is not self-reflective, meaning that A has no proper Σ^0_1-elementary substructure isomorphic to A, then A has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a finite generating tuple g for A, and let p be the atomic type of g. Any tuple g′ satisfying p generates a substructure isomorphic to A. If such a tuple g′ does not generate all of A, then the generated copy of A is a proper substructure. Since A is assumed not self-reflective, this proper copy cannot be Σ^0_1-elementary. Hence there are a tuple a from A and a quantifier-free formula ψ(x,y) such that A ⊨ ψ(g′,a), but there is no tuple b from A with A ⊨ ψ(g,b). Let S be the set of all quantifier-free formulas ψ with this property for some non-generating tuple g′ satisfying p. The Scott sentence is the conjunction of two parts. The Σ^0_2 part says: there exists a tuple x satisfying p such that for every ψ ∈ S and every tuple z, ψ(x,z) fails. The Π^0_2 part says: for every tuple x satisfying p, either x generates the whole structure, i.e. every y is equal to t(x) for some term t, or there are ψ ∈ S and z with ψ(x,z). The original A satisfies both parts using x = g. If a countable structure M satisfies the conjunction, the Σ^0_2 part gives a tuple h satisfying p and avoiding every S-witness. The Π^0_2 part then forces h to generate M, because the alternative would realize some ψ ∈ S. Since h satisfies the atomic type p of the generating tuple g, the map t(g) ↦ t(h) gives an isomorphism A ≅ M. Thus the conjunction is a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence for A.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be an X-computable finitely generated self-reflective structure. Then the relativized index set I^X(A) is Σ^0_3(X) many-one complete relative to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is given for computable A and relativizes to X. Fix an arbitrary Σ^0_3 set S, written in the form n ∈ S iff ∃e such that W_{f(e,n)} is infinite, with f computable and with the enumeration arranged so that at each stage at most one e receives a new element. Uniformly in n the construction builds a computable structure B_n such that n ∈ S implies B_n ≅ A, while n ∉ S implies B_n is not finitely generated and hence not isomorphic to A. Since A is self-reflective, there is an effective chain A_0 ≺_1 A_1 ≺_1 A_2 ≺_1 ... whose members are all isomorphic to A, each A_i is a proper Σ^0_1-elementary substructure of A_{i+1}, and the union A* is not finitely generated. The construction builds B as an increasing union of finite partial structures, viewing the language relationally, together with nested unary predicates R_0[s] ⊆ ... ⊆ R_{k_s}[s] and partial embeddings j[s]: B[s] → A* satisfying j[s](R_i[s]) ⊆ A_i. At stages 3t+1, for every active i ≤ k_s, the construction extends the copy R_i so that the range of j includes the next unused element of A_i; this ensures eventual surjectivity onto A_i. At stages 3t+2 it starts a new empty relation R_{k_s+1}. At stages 3t+3, if some W_{f(e,n)} with e < k_s receives an element, the construction collapses all current higher R_j into R_e: writing u for the tuple from R_e[s] and v for all other current elements, let ψ(u,v) be the finite atomic diagram of B[s]. Since j(u) lies in A_e and A_e ≺_1 A_{k_s}, the existential formula ∃y ψ(j(u),y), realized in A_{k_s} by j(v), is realized in A_e by some tuple a. The construction redefines j on v to a and puts all of v into R_e, setting k_{s+1}=e. This is the only place where Σ^0_1-elementarity is used. Let k = liminf_s k_s. If n ∈ S, then k is the least e for which W_{f(e,n)} is infinite; if n ∉ S, then k = ∞. For each fixed i ≤ k after all lower-priority collapses stop, R_i[t] is increasing, R_i = ⋃_t R_i[t] is a substructure of B, and j_i = ⋃_t j[t] restricted to R_i is an isomorphism R_i ≅ A_i. Also B = ⋃_{i≤k} R_i. If k is finite, infinitely many collapses to R_k ensure B = R_k ≅ A. If k = ∞, then every finite tuple of B is contained in some R_i, but R_i is properly contained in R_{i+1}; choosing an element of A_{i+1} outside A_i and pulling it back shows the tuple cannot generate B. Thus B is not finitely generated. This gives a many-one reduction from S to I(A), proving Σ^0_3-hardness; membership in Σ^0_3 follows for finitely generated computable structures from their computable Σ^0_3 Scott sentences.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every finitely generated field has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof shows that a finitely generated field F is not self-reflective, and then applies Theorem 2.1. Let F have characteristic p, possibly p = 0, and let F_p be its prime field. Write F = F_p(a_1,...,a_m,b_1,...,b_n), where a_1,...,a_m is a transcendence basis and the b_j are algebraic over F_p(a_1,...,a_m). Suppose φ: F → E is an isomorphism from F onto a proper subfield E ⊊ F. Let a_i′ = φ(a_i) and b_j′ = φ(b_j), so E = F_p(a′,b′). Since F and E have the same finite transcendence degree, the original tuple a,b is algebraic over F_p(a′,b′). Therefore the atomic type of a,b over F_p(a′,b′) is isolated by a formula ψ(a′,b′,x,y). This existential formula is realized in F by x,y = a,b. It cannot be realized in E by any c,d ∈ E, because then E(c,d) = E would be isomorphic to F over E, impossible when E is a proper subfield of F. Thus E is not Σ^0_1-elementary in F. No proper self-copy of F is Σ^0_1-elementary, so F is not self-reflective, and Theorem 2.1 yields a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 (Britton's Lemma)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a group, let H,K ⊆ G be subgroups, and let α: H → K be an isomorphism. The HNN extension of G by α is G*_α = ⟨S,t | R, t h t^{-1} = α(h) for all h ∈ H⟩. If a word w in G*_α has normal form w = g_0 t^{e_1} g_1 t^{e_2} ... t^{e_n} g_n with each g_i ∈ G and each e_i ∈ {1,-1}, and if w represents the identity, then either n = 0 and g_0 is the identity in G, or there is a pinch t g_k t^{-1} with g_k ∈ H, or there is a pinch t^{-1} g_k t with g_k ∈ K. Consequently, the natural homomorphism G → G*_α is an embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes Britton's Lemma as a standard tool. Its role in the later construction is to force any nontrivial trivial word in the HNN extension either to come from the base group K or to contain a reducible subword t u t^{-1} or t^{-1} u t where u belongs to the appropriate associated subgroup generated by the b_i. This lets the authors combine HNN normal-form reductions with small-cancellation reductions inside the base group.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4 (Greendlinger's Lemma)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = ⟨S | R⟩ be a group with a symmetrized presentation satisfying the small-cancellation condition C′(λ), where 0 ≤ λ ≤ 1/6. If w is a nontrivial freely reduced word representing the identity in G, then some cyclic permutation r of a relator from R or its inverse can be written r = u v such that u is a subword of w and |u| > (1 - 3λ)|r|. In particular, for a C′(1/6) presentation, a Dehn-minimal word, meaning a word containing no subword longer than half of a relator, represents the identity if and only if it is the empty word.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses Greendlinger's Lemma as the small-cancellation engine. If a nonempty freely reduced word equals the identity, then it has a long overlap with some defining relator. Replacing that long overlap by the complementary shorter part of the relator strictly decreases word length. Iterating this yields Dehn's algorithm: reduce until no more long relator subwords occur; the resulting Dehn-minimal word is trivial exactly when the original word was trivial. This reduction principle is combined with Britton's Lemma to solve the word problem in the constructed HNN extension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4.3 small-cancellation observation for K
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be the directed tree with vertices V(T) = {(n,τ) : n ∈ ω and τ ∈ Z^{<ω}}, with parent (n,τ)^- = (n,τ^-) when τ is nonempty and (n,⟨⟩)^- = (n+1,⟨⟩). Let B = {b_i : i ∈ Z}, let a be another generator, and let u(x,y) = x y x^2 y ... x^{100} y. Define K with generators V(T) ∪ {a} ∪ B and relators u((n,τ),a) = (n,τ)^- and u((n,τ),b_i) = (n,τ⌢⟨i⟩), for all (n,τ) ∈ V(T) and i ∈ Z. The paper notes that this presentation is C′(1/10). It also notes that any reduced word in the letters B is Dehn-minimal, and hence B freely generates a free subgroup of K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The C′(1/10) verification is recorded without detailed proof in the paper and is based on the long rigid relators built from u(x,y). Since no relator contains two consecutive b_i letters, a reduced word using only B-letters cannot contain a subword longer than half of any relator. Thus a nonempty reduced B-word is Dehn-minimal. By Greendlinger's Lemma, a nonempty Dehn-minimal word cannot represent the identity in the C′(1/10) presentation. Therefore the subgroup generated by B is a free group on the b_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Claim 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With K as in Section 4.3, let F(B) be the subgroup generated by B = {b_i : i ∈ Z}. If v is a Dehn-minimal word in the alphabet V(T) ∪ {a} ∪ B, then the element represented by v lies in F(B) if and only if v itself is a word using only letters from B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward implication is the content. Suppose v is Dehn-minimal and represents the same element of K as a reduced B-word v′. Remove any common prefix so that v^{-1}v′ is freely reduced. If v′ is empty, then v represents the identity, so Dehn-minimality and Greendlinger's Lemma force v to be empty. Otherwise v^{-1}v′ is a nontrivial word representing the identity. Greendlinger's Lemma gives a subword u of v^{-1}v′ which is also a subword of some relator r and has length |u| > 0.7|r|, since the presentation is C′(1/10). Relators in K have no two consecutive B-letters, so the long subword u cannot lie substantially inside the B-word v′; at most its last letter can be in v′. Thus the part u′ of u lying in v^{-1} has length at least |u|-1, which is still greater than half of |r| because the relators have length greater than 100. This contradicts the assumption that v is Dehn-minimal. Hence v must have been a B-word.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the small-cancellation group from Section 4.3. Let G be the HNN extension G = ⟨K,t | t b_i t^{-1} = b_{i+1} for every i ∈ Z⟩. Then G is self-reflective. More explicitly, G is finitely generated by (0,⟨⟩), a, b_0, and t, and the subgroup H generated by (1,⟨⟩), a, b_0, and t is a proper Σ^0_1-elementary subgroup of G that is isomorphic to G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, H is isomorphic to G. Define ι on generators by ι((n,τ)) = (n+1,τ) and by fixing a, every b_i, and t. The relators are preserved: u((n+1,τ),a) = (n+1,τ)^- = ι((n,τ)^-), u((n+1,τ),b_i) = (n+1,τ⌢⟨i⟩) = ι((n,τ⌢⟨i⟩)), and t b_i t^{-1} = b_{i+1} is fixed. Thus ι is a homomorphism onto H. To prove injectivity, take a nontrivial word v of minimal length among words representing its group element and suppose ι(v)=1. By Britton's Lemma, either ι(v) lies in K or it contains a pinch t u t^{-1} or t^{-1} u t with u ∈ F(B). If a pinch occurs, minimality of v implies u is Dehn-minimal; otherwise a Dehn reduction in u would give a shorter word for v. Claim 4.5 then says u is actually a B-word, so the corresponding pinch already existed in v and could be shortened using the HNN relation, contradicting minimality. Hence no t occurs. Then Greendlinger's Lemma applied in K gives a long relator subword of ι(v); every such relator is the ι-image of a relator for v, so v itself would be Dehn-reducible, again contradicting minimality. Thus ι is injective. Second, H is proper. If (0,⟨⟩) lay in H, choose a shortest spelling v for it using the generators ι(V(T)), a, B, and t. Applying Britton's Lemma to (0,⟨⟩)^{-1}v and arguing as above removes t. Greendlinger's Lemma then gives a long relator subword. This subword cannot involve the initial (0,⟨⟩)^{-1} in the required way, because the only relevant relation containing (0,⟨⟩)^{-1} without other level-0 vertices is u((0,⟨⟩),a) = (1,⟨⟩), whose long subwords contain more than one occurrence of (0,⟨⟩). Hence the reducible subword lies inside v, contradicting shortest spelling. Third, H is Σ^0_1-elementary. It suffices to show a retraction property: for every finite set S ⊂ G with 1 ∉ S, there is a homomorphism κ: G → G with κ restricted to H equal to the identity, κ(G)=H, and 1 ∉ κ(S). Fix shortest spellings of elements of S. Choose an integer n much larger than all lengths and B-subscripts appearing, define κ by fixing the generators of H and sending (0,⟨⟩) to (1,⟨n⟩), equivalently sending each (0,τ) occurring through the tree relations to (1,⟨n⟩⌢τ). If κ(s)=1 for some s ∈ S, Britton's Lemma again either produces a forbidden HNN shortening of the original shortest spelling or leaves a word in K. Greendlinger's Lemma then gives a long relator subword in κ(s). Because n is large, any relator involving the new branch (1,⟨n⟩⌢σ) corresponds exactly to a relator involving (0,σ) in s, and any relator not involving the new branch is already present unchanged in s. In all cases one obtains a shorter spelling of s, contradiction. This retraction property preserves every finite quantifier-free diagram over H with inequalities, so H ≺_1 G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The finitely generated group G constructed in Lemma 4.6 is computable; equivalently, its word problem with respect to the given recursive presentation is decidable, yielding a computable copy of G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The algorithm takes a word in the generators V(T) ∪ {a} ∪ B ∪ {t} and repeatedly applies length-decreasing replacements of three kinds. First, if a subword has the form t v t^{-1} where v is a word only in the b_i, replace it by the corresponding shifted B-word using the HNN relation t b_i t^{-1} = b_{i+1}. Second, if a subword has the form t^{-1} v t where v is a B-word, replace it by the inverse shift. Third, if a subword v is longer than half of a relator r from the K-presentation, replace v by the complementary part of r, giving a shorter equivalent word. For a fixed input word, only finitely many K-relators can be relevant to the third kind of replacement, because the input mentions only finitely many letters; hence each search step is finite. All replacements strictly shorten the word, so the process terminates. If the terminal word is empty, the input was trivial. Conversely, suppose the input represented the identity but the terminal word w were nonempty. No third-kind reduction is possible, so the K-parts are Dehn-minimal. Britton's Lemma would then require an HNN pinch t v t^{-1} or t^{-1} v t with v in F(B); by Claim 4.5, the relevant v is a B-word, so a first- or second-kind reduction would have been possible. This contradiction proves correctness of the word-problem algorithm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists a finitely generated computable group G whose index set I(G) is Σ^0_3 many-one complete.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The group G is the HNN/small-cancellation group constructed in Section 4. Lemma 4.6 proves that G is self-reflective, and Proposition 4.10 proves that G is computable. Applying Theorem 2.2 to this computable self-reflective finitely generated structure gives Σ^0_3 many-one hardness of I(G). The standard Σ^0_3 upper bound comes from the known fact, recalled in the paper from Knight and Saraph, that every computable finitely generated structure has a computable Σ^0_3 Scott sentence, together with Proposition 1.2. Hence I(G) is Σ^0_3 many-one complete.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be the self-reflective group constructed in Section 4, and let R be any finitely generated ring. Then the group ring R[G], consisting of finitely supported functions G → R with the usual group-ring operations, is self-reflective.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Any endomorphism α of G induces a ring endomorphism α_* of R[G] by sending each group basis element g to α(g) and fixing the coefficient ring R. If α is injective, then α_* is injective. Let ι: G → H ⊊ G be the injective self-embedding from Lemma 4.6, with H a proper Σ^0_1-elementary copy of G. Then ι_* embeds R[G] into B = R[H], a proper subring isomorphic to R[G]. To prove B ≺_1 R[G], use the same finite-retraction criterion as for groups. Let T be a finite set of nonzero elements of R[G]. Let U be the finite set of group elements appearing in the supports of elements of T, and set S = {u_1 u_2^{-1} : u_1,u_2 ∈ U and u_1 ≠ u_2}. Since 1 ∉ S, Lemma 4.6 provides a group retraction κ: G → G with κ|H = id_H, κ(G)=H, and 1 ∉ κ(S). The last condition means κ is injective on U. The induced ring endomorphism β = κ_* is a retraction R[G] → B fixing B. Because κ is injective on the supports of the chosen elements, β cannot collapse two distinct support terms of any t ∈ T; coefficients are unchanged, so β(t) ≠ 0 whenever t ≠ 0. Thus every finite existential diagram over B realized in R[G] is realized in B, so B is a proper Σ^0_1-elementary subring isomorphic to R[G].

proof_techniques.name: Characterizing low Scott complexity by absence of existential self-copies
proof_techniques.description: For finitely generated structures, the paper converts the model-theoretic obstruction of a proper Σ^0_1-elementary self-copy into the exact dividing line for d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentences. If every proper generated copy of the structure fails to be existentially elementary, then each non-generating tuple satisfying the generator's atomic type has a finite quantifier-free witness ψ showing this failure. Collecting all such witnesses yields a Scott sentence saying that some generator-like tuple avoids all witnesses, and that any generator-like tuple either generates or realizes a witness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is relevant whenever one tries to use a finite base or finite generating tuple to control automorphisms: the paper identifies when the orbit of that finite tuple is low-complexity definable. It suggests that finitely generated candidates may be too rigidly controlled by finite parameter images for the desired nondefinability phenomenon.

proof_techniques.name: Index-set hardness from self-reflection via collapse construction
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that a self-reflective finitely generated computable structure has Σ^0_3-complete index set by building, for each Σ^0_3 predicate ∃e W_{f(e,n)} infinite, a computable structure B_n along a chain A_0 ≺_1 A_1 ≺_1 ... of self-copies. Active approximations R_i copy A_i. When W_{f(e,n)} receives evidence, all higher levels are collapsed into R_e using Σ^0_1-elementarity to preserve the finite atomic diagram. If some least e acts infinitely often, the limit is a single copy of A; if no e acts infinitely often, the limit is the non-finitely generated union of the chain.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This construction is a general-purpose way to exploit proper existential self-similarity to encode high descriptive complexity. A solver could look for analogous self-similar structures whose automorphism graphs or definability properties can be forced by controlled collapses.

proof_techniques.name: Retraction criterion for Σ^0_1-elementarity
proof_techniques.description: In the group and group-ring constructions, the authors prove H ≺_1 G by showing a stronger finite preservation property: for every finite set S of nonidentity elements, there is a retraction κ from the big structure onto the candidate substructure H, fixing H pointwise, such that κ keeps every element of S nontrivial. This ensures that any finite system of equations and inequations with parameters from H that is solvable in G remains solvable in H.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This criterion could be useful for constructing structures with controlled existential behavior while retaining enough maps to manage automorphisms. It gives a concrete way to verify existential elementarity without directly analyzing all existential formulas.

proof_techniques.name: Small cancellation plus HNN extension for computable self-similar groups
proof_techniques.description: The paper builds a group from a tree-coded small-cancellation base K and then adds an HNN stable letter t shifting a free subgroup generated by b_i. The small-cancellation relations create a rigid tree of generators, while the HNN extension makes all b_i accessible from a single b_0 and gives finite generation. Britton's Lemma controls words involving t, and Greendlinger's Lemma controls words in the base group, allowing the authors to prove injectivity, properness, retraction properties, and decidability of the word problem.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a group-theoretic example is desired, this is a template for combining finite generation, computability, and strong self-embedding/retraction features. The same machinery might be adapted to prescribe or constrain automorphisms while keeping the presentation computable.

proof_techniques.name: Dehn-minimal isolation of a free subgroup
proof_techniques.description: Claim 4.5 isolates the subgroup generated by the b_i inside the small-cancellation group K: a Dehn-minimal word represents an element of the B-subgroup only if the word itself uses only B-letters. The proof uses the fact that relators have no adjacent B-letters, so a long Greendlinger overlap cannot straddle significantly into a reduced B-word; otherwise it would contradict Dehn-minimality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This kind of subgroup isolation is useful when a construction needs a distinguished free part whose elements can be recognized from normal forms. It could help in examples where automorphisms or self-embeddings act by shifts on a controlled free subgroup.

proof_techniques.name: Algebraic type isolation to rule out self-reflection
proof_techniques.description: For finitely generated fields, the paper rules out proper Σ^0_1-elementary self-copies by using finite transcendence bases and algebraicity. If F is isomorphic to a proper subfield E, then the original generators of F are algebraic over the image generators in E, so their atomic type over E is isolated by a finite algebraic formula realized in F but not in E. This gives an existential formula witnessing that E is not existentially elementary.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For algebraic or geometric classes with a dimension theory, this technique can show that proper self-copies cannot be existentially elementary. Such rigidity may imply finite-tuple orbit definability and may obstruct attempts to obtain the original problem's nondefinability requirement in those classes.

proof_techniques.name: Transferring self-reflection through group rings
proof_techniques.description: The group-ring argument lifts a self-reflective group G to a self-reflective ring R[G]. The key is that a group retraction preserving nonidentity of all quotients u_1u_2^{-1} from a finite support set is injective on that support set, so the induced ring map cannot collapse nonzero finite linear combinations. This converts finite nontriviality preservation in the group into finite nonzero preservation in the ring.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a way to move examples from groups to richer algebraic languages while preserving existential self-similarity. If one finds a group with relevant automorphism/definability behavior, group-ring constructions may transfer part of that behavior to rings.

proof_techniques.name: Using generator-tuple orbit definability as a bridge between Scott sentences and automorphisms
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses, via Alvir-Knight-McCoy, the equivalence between d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentences and Π^0_1 definability of the orbit of a generating tuple in finitely generated structures. Although the paper's main goal is Scott complexity rather than automorphism graphs, orbit definability of generators is exactly the kind of finite-data statement that determines isomorphisms and automorphisms in finitely generated structures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, this points to a natural dichotomy: if a finite tuple generates the structure, an automorphism is determined by the image of that tuple, so finite-parameter definability of automorphisms may be hard to avoid. Conversely, examples satisfying the original question likely need to avoid such finite-generation control or make every finite tuple non-basing.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Alvir, Knight, and McCoy [AKM] for the result that, for finitely generated structures, having a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence is equivalent to Π^0_1 definability of the orbit of a generating tuple. It also recalls Knight and Saraph [KS16], who observed that every computable finitely generated structure has a computable Σ^0_3 Scott sentence. The paper does not directly study computable AUT-countability or definability of individual automorphism graphs; its relevance is through finite generator orbits, self-reflective substructures, and index-set/Scott-complexity obstructions. Open questions listed in the paper include whether every finitely presented group with solvable word problem has a d-Σ^0_2 Scott sentence, whether every commutative ring has one, and whether every integral domain has one.
why_relevant: Analyzes when finitely generated structures have optimal Sigma-3 or d-Sigma-2 Scott sentences in terms of definability of generating-tuple orbits. This is relevant if one tries to use a finite base for a countable automorphism group to define automorphisms from images of that base.
